text.title: Django Import Antipatterns
text.summary: Import mistakes that cause name collisions, circular dependencies, or migration breakage in Django multi-app projects.

antipattern.description: Direct cross-app model import: from apps.users.models import User.
antipattern.reason: In projects with multiple apps each defining a User model, direct imports create silent name collisions. The alias convention makes the source app explicit at every usage and enables reliable tooling.
Wildcards make namespace scope opaque to tools (mypy, pylint, IDEs), prevent refactoring automation, and collapse the alias convention's disambiguation benefit.
Re-exports collapse the app namespace and recreate the original collision problem. Consumers can import from the wrong location silently. Keep __init__.py empty unless explicitly justified.

antipattern.description: DRF serializer Meta.model gated under TYPE_CHECKING.
antipattern.reason: Serializer Meta.model is resolved at class creation time (import time). A TYPE_CHECKING-gated import makes the symbol undefined at runtime, causing an AttributeError on serializer load. Use apps.get_model() or a runtime import for Meta.model.

antipattern.description: Agent rewrites import aliases but misses call sites: User.objects.get() not updated to user_models.User.objects.get().
antipattern.reason: Partial rewrites leave dangling names that cause NameError at runtime. Alias fixes require AST-based rewrite of both the import and every usage, not just the import line.

antipattern.description: Inconsistent alias names across files: user_models in one file, users_models in another.
antipattern.reason: Inconsistency defeats the readability benefit of the convention. Every new contributor must re-discover the alias for each app. Pin one suffix pattern (_models, _services, _constants) project-wide.
